questions.text: The process of exchange of O2 from the atmosphere with CO2 produced by the cells is called:
questions.explanation: Breathing is the physical process of inhalation and exhalation, commonly known as respiration in a general sense.

questions.text: Tracheal tubes are the respiratory organs found in:
questions.explanation: Insects use a network of tracheal tubes to transport atmospheric air directly to the cells.

questions.text: The 'Adam's Apple' is the prominent part of the:
questions.explanation: The larynx is a cartilaginous box which helps in sound production and is often called the sound box.

questions.text: A thin cartilaginous flap that prevents the entry of food into the larynx is called:
questions.explanation: The epiglottis covers the glottis during swallowing to prevent food from entering the windpipe.

questions.text: The trachea divides into two primary bronchi at the level of the:
questions.explanation: The windpipe divides into right and left primary bronchi at the level of the 5th thoracic vertebra.

questions.text: The lungs are situated in an airtight chamber known as the:
questions.explanation: The thoracic cavity is anatomically designed so that any change in its volume reflects in the lung volume.

questions.text: Expiration occurs when the intra-pulmonary pressure is:
questions.explanation: Air moves out of the lungs when pressure inside the lungs is greater than the outside atmospheric pressure.

questions.text: Which of the following helps in increasing the strength of inspiration and expiration?
questions.explanation: Humans have the ability to increase the strength of breathing using additional muscles in the abdomen.

questions.text: The volume of air inspired or expired during a normal respiration is called:
questions.explanation: Tidal Volume (TV) is approximately 500 mL in a healthy man.

questions.text: The volume of air remaining in the lungs even after a forcible expiration is:
questions.explanation: Residual Volume (RV) averages 1100 mL to 1200 mL and cannot be measured by simple spirometry.

questions.text: Vital Capacity (VC) is equal to:
questions.explanation: VC is the maximum volume of air a person can breathe in after a forced expiration.

questions.text: Partial pressure of oxygen (pO2) in the alveoli is approximately:
questions.explanation: The pO2 in alveoli (104 mm Hg) is higher than in deoxygenated blood (40 mm Hg), allowing O2 to diffuse into blood.

questions.text: Solubility of CO2 is ________ times higher than that of O2.
questions.explanation: Due to higher solubility, the amount of CO2 that can diffuse through the membrane is much higher than O2 for the same pressure difference.

questions.text: In the blood, the majority of oxygen is transported:
questions.explanation: About 97% of O2 is transported by RBCs as oxyhemoglobin.

questions.text: About 70% of CO2 is transported as:
questions.explanation: Most CO2 is converted to bicarbonate (HCO3-) in RBCs with the help of the enzyme carbonic anhydrase.

questions.text: The Oxygen dissociation curve is ________ in shape.
questions.explanation: A sigmoid curve is obtained when percentage saturation of hemoglobin with O2 is plotted against the pO2.

questions.text: Which factor shifts the oxygen dissociation curve to the right?
questions.explanation: High pCO2, high H+ concentration (low pH), and high temperature favor the dissociation of oxygen from hemoglobin.

questions.text: The respiratory rhythm center is primarily located in the:
questions.explanation: The medulla region of the brain contains the specialized center responsible for the regulation of respiratory rhythm.

questions.text: The pneumotaxic center, which can moderate the functions of the respiratory rhythm center, is in the:
questions.explanation: The pneumotaxic center in the pons can reduce the duration of inspiration and thereby alter the respiratory rate.

questions.text: Difficulty in breathing causing wheezing due to inflammation of bronchi and bronchioles is:
questions.explanation: Asthma is an allergic reaction resulting in the narrowing and inflammation of the airway.

questions.text: A chronic disorder in which alveolar walls are damaged and respiratory surface is decreased is:
questions.explanation: Emphysema is majorly caused by cigarette smoking, leading to loss of elasticity in the alveolar walls.

questions.text: Which enzyme catalyzes the formation of carbonic acid from CO2 and water in RBCs?
questions.explanation: Carbonic anhydrase is found in very high concentration in RBCs and facilitates the CO2 + H2O ⇌ H2CO3 reaction.

questions.text: The diffusion membrane at the alveoli is made up of how many layers?
questions.explanation: The three layers are: thin squamous epithelium of alveoli, endothelium of alveolar capillaries, and the basement substance between them.

questions.text: Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) includes:
questions.explanation: FRC is the volume of air that will remain in the lungs after a normal expiration.

questions.text: Every 100 mL of deoxygenated blood delivers approximately ________ of CO2 to the alveoli.
questions.explanation: Under normal physiological conditions, 4 mL of CO2 is released for every 100 mL of blood.

questions.text: Every 100 mL of oxygenated blood can deliver around ________ of O2 to the tissues.
questions.explanation: Normal oxygenated blood carries about 20 mL of O2 per 100 mL, but only 5 mL is typically released to tissues.

questions.text: Occupational Respiratory Disorders like Silicosis are common in which industry?
questions.explanation: Long-term exposure to dust in these industries leads to fibrosis (proliferation of fibrous tissues) and serious lung damage.

questions.text: The chemosensitive area adjacent to the rhythm center is highly sensitive to:
questions.explanation: Increase in CO2 and H+ ions activates this center, which then signals the rhythm center to make adjustments.

questions.text: Receptors associated with the aortic arch and carotid artery can also recognize changes in:
questions.explanation: These peripheral chemoreceptors primarily respond to CO2 and pH changes, sending signals to the rhythm center.

questions.text: The role of oxygen in the regulation of respiratory rhythm is:
questions.explanation: The respiratory center is much more sensitive to CO2 levels than to O2 levels under normal conditions.

questions.text: Pleural fluid found between the two pleural membranes serves to:
questions.explanation: Pleural fluid acts as a lubricant, allowing the lungs to slide easily against the thoracic wall during breathing.

questions.text: During inspiration, the diaphragm:
questions.explanation: Contraction of the diaphragm increases the volume of the thoracic chamber in the antero-posterior axis.

questions.text: The conducting part of the respiratory system starts from the external nostrils and ends at the:
questions.explanation: The conducting part transports air, clears it of foreign particles, and humidifies it; exchange occurs only in alveoli/ducts.

questions.text: A healthy human breathes ________ times per minute.
questions.explanation: The average resting respiratory rate for an adult is 12 to 16 breaths per minute.

questions.text: Carbonic anhydrase is found in:
questions.explanation: It is found in very high concentrations in RBCs and in minute quantities in the plasma.

questions.text: The binding of oxygen with hemoglobin is primarily related to:
questions.explanation: Binding is primarily dependent on pO2; other factors like pCO2 and H+ concentration interfere with it.

questions.text: The total volume of air a person can expire after a normal inspiration is:
questions.explanation: EC = TV + ERV.

questions.text: Which part of the respiratory system is responsible for the actual diffusion of O2 and CO2?
questions.explanation: The exchange part includes the alveoli and their ducts.

questions.text: What is the approximate partial pressure of CO2 (pCO2) in deoxygenated blood?
questions.explanation: pCO2 is 45 mm Hg in deoxygenated blood and 40 mm Hg in alveoli/oxygenated blood.

questions.text: The external intercostal muscles help in increasing the thoracic volume in the ________ axis.
questions.explanation: The diaphragm increases volume in the antero-posterior axis, while intercostals increase it in the dorso-ventral axis.
